text:
PARIS 芒聙聰 Canada sent women芒聙聶s soccer coach Bev Priestman home from the Olympics Friday, though I have to assume that as I type this, she is reading it. An analyst in Priestman芒聙聶s program wascaught using a drone to film an opponent芒聙聶s practice, which spurred a damning TSNreportthat Canada芒聙聶s women芒聙聶s and men芒聙聶s teams have been doing this 芒聙聹for years,芒聙聺 which led to Priestman leaving the defending gold medalists, which could lead to her getting fired. The analyst, Joseph Lombardi, and assistant coach Jasmine Mander were also sent home as a result.

Every Olympics has a few scandals. That comes with the territory. The Olympic motto should be Citius Altius Fortius Bribe Us. But as scandals go, this is shaping up to be a big one芒聙聰bigger than it probably seems to American fans who are accustomed to videotaping scandals.

Last fall, Michigan芒聙聶s football team was caughttaking video of upcoming opponents芒聙聶 sidelines. It was against the rules, but it happened in packed stadiums; watching the signals was legal, but taking video of them was not. Those transgressions would have been exponentially worse if Michigan had been caught breaking into opponents芒聙聶 practices and filming them.

That is what Canada did this week芒聙聰and, according to TSN, before the Tokyo Olympics as well. That is a really significant tactical advantage. Priestman芒聙聶s team won gold in Tokyo by the thinnest of margins, with a relentlessly disciplined style. Canada allowed just four goals in six games. None of them came on corners or free kicks芒聙聰set pieces where spying on an opponents芒聙聶 practice would help the most. Canada beat Brazil in the quarterfinal and Sweden in the final in penalty shootouts.

The IOC has stripped medals from many athletes long after awarding them. Usually, it芒聙聶s for doping. But in 2008, Swedish wrestler Ara Abrahamian threw his bronze medal to the ground and went after an official, and the IOC took his medal away for violating the spirit of fair play.

If Canada videotaped its opponents芒聙聶 practices on its way to gold 芒聙娄 well 芒聙娄

We芒聙聶re not there yet. We might not get there. But this is a stain on the Canada Soccer Association, and it芒聙聶s not going away.

Canada is now battling on two fronts: What happened, and what it meant.

A thorough, transparent investigation would tell us what happened芒聙聰and there is a reasonable chance we will get one. Canada Soccer芒聙聶s CEO, Jason Blue, has already acknowledged that: 芒聙聹Over the past 24 hours, additional information has come to our attention regarding previous drone use against opponents, predating the Paris 2024 Olympic Games,芒聙聺 and promised an independent review. Cynicism in these situations is understandable. But Blue has only been on the job for five months. If he did not know Canada was cheating, then the worst thing he can do for his own job security is cover it up.

Whatever that investigation reveals, there will be a lengthy debate about what it meant.

This is where other scandals are instructive. The New England Patriots went against the NFL芒聙聶s rules on the videotaping of opponents芒聙聶 sidelines芒聙聰violations that became particularly brazen after the league sent a memo specifically warning teams not to do it. Within the NFL, a lot of people believed the Pats cheated their way to their first three Super Bowl wins. Those feelings grew stronger as New England failed to win another, and even stronger after the team was allegedly caught deflating footballs. But then New England won three more Super Bowls; an effective rebuttal.

In 2019, the Houston Astros were caught illegally taking video of opponents芒聙聶 signs. That tainted the team芒聙聶s 2017 World Series championship. But since that scandal broke, the Astros have played in four straight American League Championship Series and two World Series, and they won the title in '22. That, too, is an effective rebuttal.

After Michigan芒聙聶s sign-stealing was exposed, the Wolverines went on to win the national championship芒聙聰another effective rebuttal.

This year芒聙聶s Canadian women芒聙聶s team carries an enormous burden to validate the 2021 gold medal. It isn芒聙聶t fair or completely logical, especially since Canada芒聙聶s all-time best player, Christine Sinclair, has retired from international play. But public perception is rarely fair.

Priestman is gone. Canada芒聙聶s women芒聙聶s soccer team is still here. Suspicion will hang over both, for the rest of these Olympics芒聙聰and for a long, long time after they end.

Michael Rosenberg is a senior writer for Sports Illustrated, covering any and all sports. He writes columns, profiles and feature stories and has covered almost every major sporting event. He joined SI in 2012 after working at the Detroit Free Press for 13 years, eight of them as a columnist. Rosenberg is the author of 芒聙聹War As They Knew It: Woody Hayes, Bo Schembechler and America in a Time of Unrest.芒聙聺 Several of his stories also have been published in collections of the year芒聙聶s best sportswriting. He is married with three children.

title: All Eyes Are on Canada Soccer at the Olympics As Drone Spying Scandal Develops
description: Head coach Bev Priestman was sent home from Paris after new allegations emerged, citing 芒聙聹years芒聙聺 of filming opponents芒聙聶 practices by both the women芒聙聶s and men芒聙聶s teams.
